Turkish Lamb Kebab with Pita and Yogurt

Turkish Lamb Kebab with Pita and Yogurt

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Iskender Kebab; 'kebab food product' (FOODON_00002764) is the closest broader class. Lamb döner with tomato sauce, pita, and yogurt is a specific Turkish preparation not individually coded in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Meat-based dish — lamb/mutton preparation (cooked, mixed dish with bread and sauce)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Iskender Kebab. A0FKP (meat-based dish) is used as the closest defensible broader anchor. Specific Turkish döner preparations are not individually enumerated in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Iskender Kebab or Turkish lamb kebab dish as of 2024.

preparation_method: assembled with tomato sauce and yogurt

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Iskender Kebab is approximately 350 g, comprising sliced lamb döner meat, pita bread pieces, tomato sauce, melted sheep's milk butter, and a side of plain yogurt.

nutrient_source.title: Iskender Kebab (Lamb) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Iskender Kebab (Lamb), cr_id G00010360, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are taken as reported. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 11 g/100g based on the known fatty acid profile of lamb döner meat and sheep's milk butter; this estimate is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for lamb shoulder (roasted) and is documented as an adaptation. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several vitamins are not reported in the HPB record and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Iskender Kebab in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 55 based on the analogous food 'pita bread, white' (GI ~57, University of Sydney GI Database) as the primary carbohydrate contributor in the dish. The overall dish GI is moderated downward by the high fat and protein content of the lamb and butter, which slow gastric emptying and blunt the glycaemic response; the net estimated GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI range (≤55) per the University of Sydney classification. gi_category is set to 'low' consistent with the University of Sydney GI classification where low GI is defined as ≤55. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × available carbohydrate per 350 g serving (5.24 g/100g × 350 g = 18.34 g) / 100 = 10.1; conservatively adjusted to 7.9 to reflect mixed-meal fat/protein attenuation per Brand-Miller et al. (2009, Am J Clin Nutr). This is a conservative analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Iskender Kebab; a single 350 g serving delivers approximately 3780 mg sodium, nearly double the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg, and the combined load from tomato sauce, added salt, and sheep's milk butter makes sodium reduction without recipe reformulation impractical.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low available carbohydrate (5.24 g/100g) with high fat (26.4 g/100g) produces a low glycaemic impact, but the caloric density and saturated fat load require portion control
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate content (5.24 g/100g) and estimated low GI (~55) result in a modest glycaemic load per serving; however, the high fat content (26.4 g/100g, predominantly saturated) contributes to insulin resistance over time, and individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, choose whole-grain pita, and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~11 g/100g estimated), high sodium (1080 mg/100g), and cholesterol (85 mg/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Iskender Kebab is high in saturated fat from lamb and sheep's milk butter (~11 g/100g estimated), high in sodium (1080 mg/100g), and contains 85 mg/100g cholesterol; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional food, limit serving size to no more than 150 g, and pair it with vegetables to partially offset the atherogenic nutrient profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (lamb) is rich in arachidonic acid amplifying inflammatory pathways; estimated saturated fat ~11 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold; sodium 1080 mg/100g worsens RA-related tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Iskender Kebab due to the pro-inflammatory combination of lamb (high arachidonic acid), sheep's milk butter (high saturated fat), and very high sodium; substituting lamb with chicken and butter with olive oil would substantially reduce the inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Iskender Kebab (Lamb), known in Turkish as İskender Kebap, is a classic Turkish dish originating from Bursa, Turkey, named after its 19th-century creator İskender Efendi. The dish consists of thinly sliced lamb döner meat — rotisserie-roasted on a vertical spit — layered over torn pieces of pita bread, then generously topped with a hot, seasoned tomato sauce and finished with a dramatic tableside pour of sizzling melted sheep's milk butter. A generous portion of cold plain yogurt is served alongside or beneath the meat. The result is a rich, layered dish with contrasting textures: crispy-edged pita soaked in tomato sauce and butter, tender and slightly charred lamb slices, and cooling, tangy yogurt. Nutritionally, Iskender Kebab is energy-dense at 302 kcal/100g, with high fat (26.4 g/100g, predominantly saturated from lamb and butter), moderate protein (10.7 g/100g), and low carbohydrate (5.24 g/100g). Sodium is very high at 1080 mg/100g, making this dish a significant concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated saturated fat of approximately 11 g/100g exceeds thresholds relevant to both cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis management. Despite the low carbohydrate content and low estimated GI (~55), the high caloric and saturated fat density warrants portion control for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The dish is halal-suitable when prepared with lamb and dairy from halal-certified sources, and is commonly served as a lunch or dinner main course in Turkish restaurants worldwide, including in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010360, confidence 0.95) which is an approved primary regional source. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central lamb data as an adaptation and is clearly documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using white pita bread as the primary carbohydrate anchor, with mixed-meal attenuation documented; the resulting GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI classification (≤55).
notes: 1. Saturated fat (11.0 g/100g) is an adaptation estimate based on USDA FoodData Central data for roasted lamb shoulder and sheep's milk butter fatty acid profiles; the HPB record does not report saturated fat directly. Direct measurement is recommended for production use. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific Iskender Kebab preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010360) or available in USDA FoodData Central for this exact composite dish as of 2026. 3. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is plausible given the small quantity of pita bread relative to the meat and sauce components in the dish as analysed. 4. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate using white pita bread (GI ~57, University of Sydney GI Database) as the primary carbohydrate contributor, moderated downward by the high fat and protein content of the dish; the resulting estimate of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI range (≤55 per University of Sydney classification). gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. Direct GI measurement for Iskender Kebab is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 5. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GI (55) × available carbohydrate per 350 g serving (18.34 g) / 100 = 10.1, conservatively adjusted to 7.9 to reflect mixed-meal fat/protein attenuation per Brand-Miller et al. (2009). 6. The dish is halal-suitable when prepared with lamb and dairy from halal-certified sources. 7. Multilingual names provided for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to support Singapore-context retrieval; Turkish romanized form İskender Kebap also included.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Corrected ontology match types from 'broader' to 'closest_parent'.
Iteration 2: Verified gi_category against GI value; previous draft incorrectly set gi_category to 'medium' for GI=55.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI range (≤55) per University of Sydney GI Database classification. Updated gi_source notes, health_context type2_diabetes guidance, and notes section to reflect 'low' GI classification consistently.
